segments.title: HMS Tiger and the battlecruiser trade-off
segments.summary: HMS Tiger shows how British battlecruisers pursued heavy guns and 28-knot speed while accepting less protection than a battleship.
segments.body: Clarke places Tiger alongside the contemporary Iron Duke and Queen Elizabeth programmes to explain why her 13.5-inch guns were not an unusual choice. Her machinery and layout supported an exceptionally fast, well-armed ship, but British battlecruiser design still favoured speed and firepower over armour. That balance shaped what such ships could be asked to do in fleet action.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses Tiger's guns, boilers, speed, armour belt, and British battlecruiser priorities.

segments.title: Normandy's quadruple-turret compromise
segments.summary: France's Normandy class concentrated twelve 13.4-inch guns in quadruple turrets, but its 21-knot speed and unusual layout raised hard questions about the design's value.
segments.body: The Normandy design illustrates that a larger broadside did not automatically produce a convincing battleship. Clarke weighs its triple quadruple-turret arrangement, modest speed, and protection against the ships its opponents were building. None of the class entered service as battleships, which makes the design a useful case in the limits of paper comparison.

evidence.note: The transcript examines Normandy's quadruple turrets, gun count, speed, protection, and uncompleted service history.

segments.title: Pennsylvania's twelve-gun answer
segments.summary: The Pennsylvania class used four triple 14-inch turrets to offer a heavier broadside than a Queen Elizabeth without waiting for a new gun or turret design.
segments.body: Clarke treats the American response as an evolution of technology already in hand: four triple turrets could be fitted where earlier ships mixed triple and twin mounts. The result traded speed against a powerful broadside, creating a different tactical problem for a faster British opponent. It shows how an established industrial and technical base can make an apparently sudden improvement practical.

evidence.note: The transcript describes the Pennsylvania class's four triple 14-inch turrets, broadside, existing turret technology, and speed trade-off.

segments.title: Bayern, Revenge, and fleet numbers
segments.summary: Comparing the Bayern and Revenge classes shows why capital-ship competition involved speed, armour, gun calibre, and the number of hulls a navy could afford.
segments.body: Clarke argues that matching Queen Elizabeth's eight 15-inch guns at roughly 21 knots did not by itself create an equivalent challenge, because the faster British ships could choose the terms of an encounter. He then links the American choice not to jump to 16-inch guns with Britain's ability to build the more numerous Revenge class. Turret choice also mattered: cheaper twin mounts could support a larger fleet than more ambitious multiple-gun installations.

evidence.note: The transcript compares the German response, Queen Elizabeth and Revenge classes, American gun choices, armour, speed, and turret cost.

segments.title: Salamis and the limits of international supply
segments.summary: The unfinished Salamis reveals how a warship programme could be stranded when hull construction, heavy guns, and international supply chains did not align.
segments.body: Salamis was being assembled while its guns depended on American delivery, leaving a critical gap once war intervened. Clarke uses the case to separate an impressive hull from a completed fighting ship: industrial capacity and international access to armament were both decisive. Its guns later found a different use on Abercrombie-class monitors, underlining how wartime priorities could redirect scarce equipment.

evidence.note: The transcript explains that Salamis depended on American guns and connects the undelivered armament to Abercrombie-class monitors.

segments.title: Why the 1913 naval race was global
segments.summary: The 1913 capital-ship race reached far beyond Britain and Germany, with American, Japanese, Spanish, French, and other programmes shaping the wider contest.
segments.body: Clarke cautions against treating the dreadnought race as a bilateral contest when several navies were developing ships and technologies at the same time. He also frames Tiger as a ship for protecting worldwide trade and pursuing enemy cruisers and battlecruisers, not merely for a North Sea duel. The wider strategic mission helps explain why each navy made different choices about speed, armament, and purpose.

evidence.note: The transcript identifies the multinational character of the race and explains Tiger's global trade-protection role.

segments.title: American deterrence without escalation
segments.summary: Pennsylvania combined regional deterrence with a 12-gun broadside, while British and American firms still shared key turbine and boiler ideas.
segments.body: Clarke presents the American programme as an assertion of naval status and regional security, shaped in part by South American battleship construction, rather than as a direct demand to rival Britain everywhere. He notes that turbine and boiler technologies moved between the two countries even as their fleets were compared. The result was a credible American force without an immediate leap to still larger guns.

evidence.note: The transcript discusses American regional naval status, South American programmes, technology exchange, and Pennsylvania's 12-gun deterrent.

segments.title: Capital ships and the uncertainty of deterrence
segments.summary: By 1913, fleets were ordering powerful ships to deter rivals without knowing how soon war would test whether their force was strong enough.
segments.body: Britain's planned Queen Elizabeth and Revenge construction promised a formidable concentration of 15-inch-gun battleships, but every navy still faced uncertainty about future threats. Clarke stresses that governments believed they had time and that their programmes were winning the deterrence contest. The real strategic question was whether a fleet was sufficiently powerful to prevent a crisis from becoming a war.

evidence.note: The transcript connects planned British 15-inch battleships with the uncertainty of pre-war deterrence and approaching conflict.
